data.title: John Hancock 2055 Lifetime Blend Portfolio Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review and outlook

Financial assets delivered strong returns in the quarter, with both stocks and bonds registering gains. A favorable backdrop of positive global growth, falling interest rates across the developed markets, and a lack of pronounced negative news fueled a surge in investors’ appetite for risk. Most notably, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates by a quarter point in September, and the markets anticipated that additional easing could be in store over the months ahead. Investors were further cheered by positive corporate earnings reports and the relatively limited initial economic impact from tariffs.

These developments helped propel major equity indexes to a series of all-time highs. The United States led the way higher behind renewed enthusiasm about the artificial intelligence theme and the technology sector more broadly. Small-cap stocks, which benefited from the improving interest rate outlook and investors’ preference for risk, performed particularly well. The emerging markets were another top performer, with China registering an impressive gain amid revived optimism about the country’s economic outlook.

The global fixed-income markets also produced positive returns, reflecting the increasingly favorable shift in Fed policy. Longer-term debt and credit-oriented categories generally provided the best results.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation contributed to the fund’s relative performance in the quarter. An overweight in equities and corresponding underweight in bonds were the primary contributors to third-quarter relative performance. An overweight in emerging-market bonds in the fixed-income portfolio was a further positive. An underweight in emerging-market stocks detracted, as did an overweight in U.S. mid caps.

Portfolio changes

Investment conditions remained broadly positive at the close of the period, highlighted by ongoing optimism related to economic conditions and global central bank policy. However, valuations have become fairly full across many areas of the equity and fixed-income markets. With this in mind, we maintain a diversified portfolio designed to hold up through a wide range of possible outcomes. We think it’s better to keep a long-term view rather than trying to chase returns in specific categories. We believe this approach should hold the fund in good stead over time, and we think its merits may become increasingly apparent if the market backdrop becomes less favorable than investors are anticipating.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 245: $24,500 Allocated, $2,722.19 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
It was a difficult week for the market as the Nasdaq fell -4.43% while the S&P 500 declined by -2.72%. There was a major tech retracement as some of the investment community started to question the valuations of some technology companies after negative economic data. As the government shutdown continues there is a delay in official reports and the market is now relying on private sector data as their economic indicators. A report from Challenger, Gray & Christmas showed that U.S. employers announced the largest number of job cuts for the month of October in over 20 years which sparked fears of a weakening labor market. We also got a report on Friday from the University of Michigan indicating that consumer sentiment had fallen sharply to its lowest level since June 2022. On Friday the government had been shut down for 38 days and there was rumors circulating that the Fed could be pushed into a situation where they wouldn’t cut in December if they didn’t have official data from the government to review. This is the first time in 245 weeks where I didn’t get my portfolio data finished on Sunday night so the portfolio numbers will reflect after Mondays increase. On Monday the market rallied as it looked like a deal to open the government was reached and the market had one of its best days of the year. I am super bullish on the rest of the year as I believe we’re heading into a strong business environment as the Fed lowers rates and we see productivity gains from A.I.

After the massive decline last week and the rally in the beginning of this week things are looking good for a year end rally after it looks like a deal has been reached to reopen the government. After allocating $24,500 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 245 at $32,274.44. The account's profitability has increased by $616.15 (8.49%) to $7,874.44 which is a return on invested capital of 32.27%. This week I added to my positions in ONEOK (OKE) and Starwood Property Trust (STWD). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $55 of dividend income in week 245. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $15.24 (0.57%) to $2,722.19. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $5,205.15 of income since its inception, which is 21.25% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. As the government reopens I think we will get a bit of a surge as more investors are likely to think a Santa Clause rally is going to occur especially since the Fed will have data to go off at the next FOMC meeting.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

After the rebound on Monday the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is at all-time highs with an account value of $32,274.44 after investing $24,500. What’s crazy is that I am now generating $2,722.19 in forward dividend income which is a portfolio yield of 8.43% and a yield on cost of 11.11%. I am currently up 31.73% or $7,774.44 on invested capital. When I think about starting this account with $100, and adding $100 per week toward income producing assets I am quite pleased with the progress. My focus was to build a portfolio that would mitigate risk while generating recurring income. I wanted capital appreciation to be a secondary goal, as I was not focused on replicating or trying to exceed the market's return. I am excited to see where this goes as I think there is a good chance I will be generating around $2,850 in forward income heading into 2026.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $978.25 (35.94%)
  • Equities $604.60 (22.21%)
  • CEFs $441.82 (16.23%)
  • REITs $436.76 (16.04%)
  • BDCs $260.76 (9.58%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 45 weeks in 2025, $2,025.47 of dividend income was produced, which is 124.79% of the total income from 2024. There were 682 positions that paid a dividend, which is 96.19% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $55 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $45.01. This is an increase of $13.80 (44.20%) YoY, and there have been 4 weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I'm really excited for the rest of October to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

October ended up breaking my previous record of $209.73 in dividend income as I generated $229.93. We’re starting off November having generated $55 in the first week. I just looked at the calendar and because of when Thanksgiving falls I am not sure what the last week in November will look like. When I look at the chart below December was the second highest month last year and I have a feeling that I am going to exceed $225 in both November and December this year. The year is winding down and I really like the way the YoY progress looks on a monthly basis and I am excited for 2026 as I think there is a lot of opportunities in the market for income producing equities.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 43 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There is $590.11 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I am making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding to REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It's difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, especially after big tech crushed earnings. I am going to work on evening out the sectors and have them more proportional in the future. Individual equities now represent 38.45% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.13% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.55% of the portfolio and generate 77.87% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) is starting to climb again as it now represents 4.06% of the portfolio. MO took a hit, but as more capital comes back into the market looking for yield I think that this Dividend King is going to become more attractive. I am happy with the way the portfolio is rounding out and as Verizon (VZ) starts to dip well below the 3% level I would consider adding more to my position. One of the keys to this portfolio's success has been the level of diversification. I am expecting the top 20 positions to change a bit next year, and I am hoping that I can get all the positions a bit more even in the future. While I have allowed myself a 5% maximum, I really want to have a well-rounded portfolio that can mitigate risk well.

None of the top-10 positions changed this week but some did pay dividends and I added to STWD. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,858.76, and they finished the week with a value of $9,031.67. This is an ROI of $2,172.91 (31.68%). There has been $1,427.31 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 20.81% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $741.95 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10.82%. These positions represent 27.98% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.26% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 244 Additions

This week I added to ONEOK (OKE), and Starwood Property Trust (STWD).

ONEOK

  • Shares of OKE are down -36/10% over the past year which has pushed the dividend yield past 6%
  • I believe pipelines are going to get rerated as new companies can’t just come in and build new pipelines at a time when more energy is needed to fuel the data center buildout
  • OKE reaffirmed its 2025 adjusted EBITDA will come in between $8 billion to $8.45 billion on the earnings call and I believe this will continue to expand and allow OKE to increase the dividend in the future
  • As income comes back into the market looking for yield I think that OKE will attract new investors and we will see shares appreciate in value

Starwood Property Trust

  • Anytime STWD is trading at the point where the yield is over 10% I start adding to my position
  • STWD has been a long-time favorite of mine when it comes to REITs as its paid the same quarterly dividend for more than a decade
  • I believe that shares of STWD will do well in a lower rate environment as all of the investments they have made will position them for a bright future

Week 246 Gameplan

It may be time to add to Verizon again and I am also keeping my eye on the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF).

Conclusion

We’re in the home stretch as the first week of November is in the books. We had a sharp decline then a miraculous reversal and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is now at all-time highs while producing $2,722.19 in forward dividend income. The S-curve is starting to occur and each month more capital gets plowed back into the portfolio. I am now reinvesting more than 50% of my monthly allocation from the dividends and I can’t wait until I am reinvesting more than 100% of my monthly allocations from the dividend income being produced. I am excited to see where I can take this portfolio as I am still very focused on building out this income stream over the coming years and before I know it I believe it will be producing over $5,000 in forward dividend income. Please leave all of your suggestions in the comment section and I enjoy corresponding with everyone.

data.title: NVR: 50-Year Mortgages To Aid Affordability Troubles?
data.description:
NVR, Inc. (NVR) is one of the U.S.’s largest homebuilders, focusing on the Mid-Atlantic, Southeast, and other regions. The company’s financials largely reflect ongoing trends in the housing market, now potentially facing a tailwind ahead – the Trump administration is considering 50-year mortgages, having noteworthy earnings implications for homebuilders. NVR’s recent results still highlight ongoing affordability concerns in the housing market.

Long-term housing market tailwinds have driven significantly good capital returns for most homebuilders. NVR’s long-term stock performance also reflects the fact, returning at a 15.9% CAGR over the past decade. NVR’s earnings growth and aggressive share buybacks have driven such high returns.

50-Year Mortgages: Aid to Homebuilders’ Earnings Slump?

The housing market could see a significant change ahead. The Trump administration is told to be working on introducing 50-year mortgages, introduced in a proposal by Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte. The addition would add a significantly longer loan term option to the current common 30-year and 15-year mortgage options.

The new mortgage term could boost housing demand; a longer loan term results in lower monthly payments, making housing more affordable for prospective homebuyers. The change would be notable especially in the current macroeconomic context - housing demand has been dampened by very weak housing affordability in the past couple of years. Housing affordability plummeted during the Covid pandemic with increasing interest rates and surging home prices, and hasn’t recovered so far. New home sales have mostly remained subdued as a result.

For homebuilders like NVR, PulteGroup (PHM), Lennar Corporation (LEN), D.R. Horton (DHI) and others, the 50-year mortgage could provide a considerable short- to mid-term boost to earnings. The longer loan term would very likely increase homebuyer demand noticeably, in turn raising NVR’s and others’ home closing volumes. The boost in demand could also indirectly cause housing prices to rise as housing supply is slow to react to higher demand; homebuilders’ margins could benefit from the higher-term mortgage. Over the long term, the positive earnings effects of the 50-year mortgage should still smooth out as industry dynamics adapt.

Ultimately, the proposal is still in its early stages – Bill Pulte has noted the 50-year mortgage to only be “a potential weapon in a wide arsenal of solutions”. The 50-year mortgage would still need the appropriate legal and regulatory changes. The administration is also considering other measures to aid the housing market, which could have similar effects. For now, the proposal is still a clear positive for homebuilders.

NVR’s Financials Reflect Industry Trends

NVR’s historical and current financial performance mainly reflects housing market dynamics. Over the long term, the company’s homebuilding revenues have grown at a 7.5% CAGR from 2015 forward and the operating margin has simultaneously improved from 12.1% to 17.9%. A longstanding housing undersupply has enabled NVR to have significant pricing power as housing prices have surged, raising the current return on capital to a great 22.9% level. Great capital returns on NVR’s growth have enabled the stock’s significant returns alongside other homebuilders.

After an overall positive earnings performance, NVR’s shorter-term performance reflects weak housing affordability. As I already noted, higher mortgage rates and other factors have caused subdued new home sales. Despite some moderation in rates in recent months, mortgage rates remain considerably high.

NVR’s Q3 results reflect the fact. Homebuilding revenues declined by -4% to $2.56 billion, reflecting a -5% decrease in home closings and a very slight year-on-year average selling price increase. Increased lot costs and limited pricing power led NVR’s gross margin to decline by 240 basis points to 21.0%, leading net income to plummet by -20% to $343 million. New orders declined by -16% to 4735 units in the quarter, still setting up especially weak expectations for the next couple of quarters.

The Q3 report underlines that the housing market still seems to be waiting for improved affordability. While countrywide home sales surged in August as mortgage rates moderated, the Q3 earnings season has again moderated forward expectations.

NVR’s Valuation Is Fair

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for NVR. The DCF model estimates NVR’s 2026 to remain challenging due to a weak backlog, but that the housing market eventually provides grounds for a small 2027-2028 revenue rebound. The rebound assumes improved affordability through lower mortgage rates and the potential for a demand boost from the 50-year mortgage proposal. I ultimately estimate growth to stabilize at 2.5%, mainly assuming modest growth in housing prices.

I also assume margins to rebound slightly alongside the housing market; I assume NVR’s EBIT margin to stabilize at 18%, slightly lower than the company has achieved through the 2021-2024 period. The estimated amount of growth shouldn’t require significant additional real estate investments from NVR, making the company’s after-tax cash flow conversion quite good in the DCF model.

The estimates put NVR’s fair value at $6960, near the stock price at the time of writing. I believe that NVR’s stock is priced with fair earnings and cash flow projections, not providing asymmetric upside or downside for now. The focus is on the housing market, and whether the 50-year mortgage or other proposals will improve housing affordability to boost demand.

NVR’s stock trades at a 10.6 EV/EBIT multiple, slightly above the largest five homebuilders’ average EV/EBIT of 8.5. While in my opinion justified by NVR’s highest capital returns of the group, I don’t believe that the multiple gives way for more upside in the valuation.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.09%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t expect NVR to leverage a noteworthy amount of debt over the long term, as the company’s debt position is currently very moderate. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.12% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. Yahoo Finance estimates NVR’s beta at 1.01. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 9.09%.

Takeaway

The housing market could see a boost ahead, if the Trump administration’s 50-year mortgage proposal or other proposals go through. Poor housing affordability continues to trouble homebuilders, reflected in NVR’s weak Q3 financials and new orders – affordability improvements are needed for homebuilders to recover into their historically very great earnings growth. NVR stock is valued fairly for now, and as such, I initiate the stock at a Hold rating.

data.title: With All Eyes On A Merger Delay, Getty Images Beats Earnings Yet Again
data.description:
I last wrote about Getty Images (GETY) in June, after Shutterstock (SSTK) shareholders approved the companies’ merger. It was also a time of some challenges for Getty, who had just missed Q1 EPS estimates on a combination of forex charges and weakness from the Palisades fire.

I rated them a buy because, while the stock was largely unchanged, there were some encouraging signs for an “off” year, that is to say, a year in which there is neither a major US election nor an Olympics to fuel editorial image demand. Despite that obvious drag, Getty was able to keep revenue more or less flat in Q1.

Here we are a few months later, and we have much to talk about. Obviously the status of the Shutterstock merger, which is likely postponed into 2026, is something to discuss, but more interesting immediate topic is the earnings, and we have two quarters to look at now.

Q2 and Q3: Encouraging Resilience for Off-Year Revenue

While forex charges continued to be a challenge in GAAP earnings in Q2, Getty was able to come in solidly ahead of earnings estimates, at least on a non-GAAP basis.

While obviously the forex loss overshadowed everything else in Q2, but for that, Getty had a very impressive result. Again, it’s an off year, and Getty not only managed to be in line with the previous year but also to slightly exceed it. If we normalize for currency losses, Getty would’ve actually been impressively profitable in Q2, especially for a year that lacks the traditional news stories that historically drove revenue growth. This became even more apparent with Q3.

Revenue in the case of Q3 was only in line with the previous year, but it’s even more impressive here because Q3 of 2024 included the 2024 Paris Olympics, a huge driver of revenue. Interesting also is that this time the forex loss was on the 2024 side, underscoring just how much it matters to GAAP earnings.

Getty’s Q3 revenue did decline on the editorial side of things, because without an Olympic Games and without a major US election cycle, it obviously would. Yet the creative side grew so much year over year it more or less made up for it, reflecting that Getty is growing beyond their heavy reliance on the news cycle to drive positive earnings.

The reasons the creative side growth is so strong take multiple forms. We’re seeing continued uptake in Getty’s generative AI offerings, though as they noted in the most recent conference call, it’s too soon to totally project how that will continue into the future. They’re conservative in their reckoning of it, but I think it’s relatively safe to say that their dalliance with generative AI is at the very least not a failure.

We’re also seeing improvement in their business with Hollywood, though they’re still not back to where they were before the 2023 strike. Here too, there is room for improvement.

Finally, we have the ongoing shift to more of a focus on subscription-based business, which continues to outperform the old model. Getty’s library is vast, and the value of having a subscription with such a company is obvious. There’s another avenue for growth that we’ve yet to see on the top or bottom line, though, and that’s also AI-related.

The Perplexity Deal

At the end of October, Getty announced a multi-year licensing deal with Perplexity across their AI-powered search efforts. Perplexity is pushing for a big share of the market with their new AI-enabled browser, Comet, and their deal with PayPal in September to give PayPal customers free trials of the Perplexity Pro service should give them a kickstart in entering the browser market in a meaningful way.

What Perplexity has done is exciting many, and now Getty has a vested interest in seeing that succeed, with integration of Getty’s API technology into Perplexity’s services. This improves Perplexity’s visual offerings and ensures attribution and origin links for Getty-owned images when they’re on display in Perplexity’s environment.

How much this will add to Getty’s revenue and how soon we’ll start to see such results are anyone’s guess, but here again it’s an encouraging sign that Getty is finding a way to make itself even more ubiquitous within an AI-focused world.

The deal helps Perplexity, and Getty should benefit from that, and it also creates a working relationship with an important up-and-coming AI brand, which could set the stage for even larger deals in the future.

But naturally, everyone following Getty’s stock has been focused on the merger with Shutterstock, so I’d be remiss if I didn’t go over where that stands.

A Merger Deferred, But Not a Merger Denied

The expectation was that Getty’s merger with Shutterstock would close by year’s end. That was potentially the most bullish aspect of this company, and of Shutterstock as well, because combined, the company would be even more of a force to be reckoned with.

The October 20 deadline for the UK’s Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) to rule on the merger saw both Getty's and Shutterstock’s stocks climb, coming as it did with little word out of the CMA. On the final day, though, the CMA extended the review of the merger to phase 2, and both stocks dropped on the news.

This is not, however, a rejection of the merger but simply an extension of the review, which both Getty and Shutterstock believe is not going to lead to a denial. It will, however, push the merger’s finalization into 2026.

That’s an annoyance but not necessarily a big problem, because unlike a lot of companies in a pending merger, neither Getty nor Shutterstock rested on their laurels waiting for the finalization but continued to grow their respective businesses, which should be all the stronger if and when the deal is finalized.

That’s important, because in the relatively unlikely case that something prevents the merger, neither company is going to be in a disastrous shape without the other. The more likely outcome, though, is still that the merger gets approved with some concessions and divestments, which the companies have already offered to make.

In my view this merger still happens; it just takes a little more time.

A Buy for the Patient Investor

So in the end, I’m going to reiterate my buy rating on Getty. I still believe that the arbitrage potential makes buying Shutterstock the more efficient way to play this merger, but Getty continues to progress as a company on its own, and the combined company should benefit greatly from the merger, but even without it, Getty is not a bad deal at this price.

Investors should worry less about the merger and keep an eye on how Getty is able to grow through their generative AI and also how the Perplexity deal starts to take shape as another avenue to get the company more consumer-facing.

In my view, the main risk here is not that the merger gets torpedoed, though that would be a near-term bearish event, but that the generative AI they’ve advanced doesn’t do as well as early indications are.

Getty’s progress as its own company is the important thing to focus on right now, as they continue to work on resolving the concerns of the CMA and finalizing the merger somewhere down the line.

data.title: Ambarella: From Auto To IoT, But Execution Is Key
data.description:
Ambarella’s (AMBA) quiet pivot from automotives-only to IoT-also is producing visible results in its topline. Revenue is up nearly 50% year over year, with IoT now contributing the bulk of growth.

The company spent the last decade positioning itself as a semiconductor company focusing on autonomous vehicles. That was a long-term vision investors bought into. Recently, though, real inflection is shifting to small, power-limited edge devices delivering both volume and pricing power.

From “Auto Optionality” to Real Revenue

For most of the last decade, Ambarella’s value proposition was tied to Level-2+ and Level-3 autonomous-driving programs that were slow to commercialize. In effect, investors were paying for potential rather than product.

Ambarella’s latest Bernstein Bus Tour presentation shows that IoT is the company’s key growth and margin driver today, representing 75% of revenue. It has expanded much faster than automotive. That means that today, Ambarella makes most of its income from IoT rather than from automotive.

IoT: the New Core Business

In IoT, Ambarella’s offerings now include much more than conventional security cameras. It offers portable video, robotics, and early edge-infrastructure appliances. Ambarella’s third gen CVflow architecture now runs these devices.

Ambarella’s CV5/CV7 SoCs are key to this transition. Both use Samsung’s 5-nm process. These chips “combine advanced image processing, high-resolution video encoding and CVflow AI processing” in a single die. This integration lets Ambarella charge higher ASPs and avoid BOM (bill of material) competition with Chinese fabs. This produces a single chip that can do many things at once - real term perception tasks including object detection, scene segmentation, gesture recognition, or AI-based editing. All this while remaining within the thermal threshold required for battery-powered drones, body-cams and sensors.

These devices - body-cams, action cameras, aerial drones, and specialized AI vision boxes - use the 5nm CV5 and CV7 SoC, the same architecture that lies under the hood of Ambarella’s automotive platform. That reuse keeps engineering costs stable while driving average selling prices higher.

In the August earnings call from Q2 FY26, we noted that there’s now a 50-50 split between price and volume in terms of year-over-year growth. In practice, that means the company’s SoC mix is shifting toward the new 5-nm chips, which already represent about 40% of total revenue. Gross margin held steady at 60.5%, at the low end of guidance. For now, the transition is helping, but whether it will sustain depends on whether IoT ASP gains overcome rising foundry costs as Ambarella ramps 5-nm volumes.

IoT has helped Ambarella raise FY26 guidance from 19-25% to 31-35%, or about $379 million midpoint. The company raised Q3 guidance to a record $100-108 million. They shipped over 36 million edge-AI processors, which helps them create a base of hundreds of customers already using their chips.

Why IoT Works Financially — and Why It Might Not Always

Automotive OEM programs used to be multi-year; so from design win to revenue took time. IoT design cycles are much shorter - it is possible to go from win to revenue in half a year. This change in the mechanics of monetization has turned R&D spending into revenue at a pace Ambarella hasn’t achieved before. But the same characteristics that make IoT a fast grower could also make it a fragile engine.

Shorter Design Cycles, Faster Monetization: In automotive, an SoC can take three to five years from design to production, given that OEM programs each need qualification, reliability testing, and safety certification. So the time between engineering and revenue recognition could mean years.

IoT changes this. A drone or body-cam design win can start generating orders within six to nine months. That is exactly what lifted fiscal 2026 guidance by over 10%. The trade-off is exposure to cyclic consumer behavior, discretionary spending behavior, and the fact that a single product’s visibility remains very short. The same speed that boosts revenue can take it off the shelf quickly.

Shared IP and Operating Leverage: That CVflow platform is common to both its IoT and automotive chips, so Ambarella can reuse much of its core IP - neural-network accelerator, image pipeline, and SDK - across both markets. This reduces R&D outlay, and maintains operating growth near 12%, even as revenue grows over 30%.

That’s great leverage, although not limitless. Newer verticals like robotics and edge-infra may require more customization, so the economies of scale may erode. But while it lasts, it is an attractive leverage.

ASP Expansion Without BOM Exposure: Ambarella’s ASP is now in the $10-$75 range thanks to the 5-nanometer CV5 and CV7 chips. That is roughly double that of its first gen video processors. Ambarella integrates AI compute, ISP and compression into one die, so it is able to compete on capability and end-results rather than on bill-of-materials (BOM) cost.

That’s a strong position to have - although it is not immune to architectural pressure. As AI workloads get heavier, die sizes and memory costs rise. Unless ASPs increase faster than foundry prices, we could see some drag on gross margin.

Working-Capital and Channel Concentration: Ambarella had a healthy product flow through distribution in Q2, having ended the quarter with 85 days of inventory, down from 98 days. But there’s one concern - WT Microelectronics, its single largest channel partner, accounted for 71% of revenue. Since WT Microelectronics operates mostly in China and Taiwan, this sort of concentration makes Ambarella vulnerable to regional slowdowns.

One more slight concern: receivables rose from 31 to 40 days, which could mean they are growing too fast for their working capital to catch up. These metrics aren’t alarming yet, just something to check in the coming quarters, because they leave little cushion if demand slows.

Cash Flow and Balance Sheet: AMBA does have $261 million in cash and almost no debt, so that does give it some room to handle volatility. However, FCF is quite thin at around $1-$5 million per quarter. What that basically means is that IoT is helping Ambarella move inventory with its faster cycles, but that hasn’t resulted in material cash generation yet. What that also means is that while IoT is monetizing Ambarella’s technology faster than automotive managed to do, it is still early days, and any disruption in inventory movement or increasing foundry cost will make trouble for that margin.

Technology and Competitive Position

AMBA’s focus - edge AI vision processing - is narrow but valuable in the semiconductor market. These are used in smaller form-factor devices that need real-time intelligence but cannot handle the power requirement or cost of GPU-class chips. So it uses its CVflow architecture to combine ISP, neural network/deep learning and video - in a single SoC. This design gives it better performance per watt than GPU-based designs in compact devices. The next generation 2-nm design (in development) extends efficiency.

Here is where Ambarella differentiates itself. Customers can be eased and locked into the CVflow ecosystem using its full software stack. Migration becomes expensive, since so many hundreds of AI models are already optimized for its SoC.

That said, competition is growing. In IoT and automotive, Qualcomm’s (QCOM) Snapdragon Vision platform is a key competitor. In robotics and ADAS, Nvidia (NVDA) with its Orin/Thor chips is dominant. The Chinese market has Horizon Robotics and Black Sesame Technologies, and Sony Group (SONY) is also integrating AI directly into its imaging sensors. So while Ambarella’s specialty remains low-power, high-integration vision SoCs, its R&D capability is just a fraction of these bigger players.

One other limitation is that Ambarella currently depends on the 5-nm process from Samsung, and it has a single dominant channel partner, as I noted earlier. This introduces supply and regional risks. So while Ambarella has its edge, that limited scale and increasing competition creates pressure.

Speaking of edge, Ambarella is actively expanding beyond IoT and into edge infra with its N-series (N1-655) processors. The target customer base is enterprise and industrial, where on-prem AI appliances are used for video analytics, logistics, and retail automation. The difference between IoT and Edge is in scale as well as economics. IoT chips are used in individual end-of-network devices like drones and cameras, whereas edge-infra processors sit one stage earlier, processing data from hundreds of such endpoint devices. That also makes them gain in ASP and system value - for perhaps a hundred lower cost endpoint devices, you have a much more expensive edge-infra chip that commands higher prices because of its multi-chip configurations and longer product lifecycles. If Ambarella can scale up its edge segment, it can support a valuation rerating that is stronger than the IoT-driven short-term momentum.

Valuation and Investment View

Ambarella, at 10x forward sales and ~87x forward EV/EBITDA, trades at a steep premium to its (slightly) larger peers. Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) trades near 17x forward sales but is far more profitable, while Semtech (SMTC) sits around 6.6x forward sales and 33x forward EV/EBITDA. Even if we consider AMBA’s faster revenue growth, this valuation is only justified if AMBA continues to grow at 30%+ CAGR and gross margins stabilize at 62-63%.

There’s a real risk of multiple compression here. AMBA has strong revenue but that doesn’t transform into strong FCF. Their EV-To-Sales multiple is over 3.6x sector median. That means, investors are already paying for a margin that they haven’t attained - yet. Their GAAP profitability is on the lower side, valuation, at 200+ Fwd PE, depends totally on future execution.

For its bull thesis to be justified, AMBA must convert its IoT business into real cash. They should also scale the edge-infrastructure business. Together, these two would support higher ASPs and longer product lifecycles. That could help AMBA sustain its 30%+ growth while expanding operating leverage by another 200 bps, which would elevate AMBA share price to the $120-130 range. That’s a 30-40% upside.

The bear case is where most of the company’s growth continues to depend on one segment (IoT') and one geographic channel (WT Microelectronics). On top of that narrow mix, foundry cost rises due to dependence on Samsung. That could take AMBA to a 6-7x sales zone, taking share price down to $70-80.

To sum up, Ambarella’s IoT pivot has improved growth but not yet profitability. Until it can generate real operating cash, its valuation will continue to look stretched.

Bottom Line

Ambarella has moved from only selling camera chips for automotive to monetizing edge AI. IoT is delivering visible growth and higher ASP along with operational synergies, but the underlying economics remains fuzzy. They have turned their R&D into revenue but not into free cash flow - yet.

AMBA’s hybrid model - volume in IoT, premium value in edge-infra - is interesting. That mix could ultimately stabilize margins and justify current valuations. However, its dependence on a single distributor, single foundry, and one dominant market (Asia') limits flexibility.

AMBA has ~10x fwd sales and 90x EV/EBITDA, which is closer to what a SaaS business has, not a semiconductor supplier. They have an ongoing transformation, but the market is paying a premium for what is still an incomplete transformation. Unless edge-infra scales up real earnings, AMBA is high-beta play. Strong technology, modest profitability.

As such, it merits a place on a watchlist, but it is too risky for a core position.

data.title: Infineon: The AI Power Conversion Wave Is Coming
data.description:
Infineon Technologies (OTCQX:IFNNY) (OTCQX:IFNNF) jumped nearly 7% after reporting strong Q4 2025 results, extending the stock’s gain to 10.9% since my last buy-rated report. While this trails both my prior 13% upside estimate and the S&P 500’s 13% return over the same period, Infineon continues to benefit from robust demand in power semiconductors and automotive applications. In this report, I break down the Q4 earnings, evaluate Infineon’s growth outlook amid elevated chip demand, and assess whether the stock still justifies a buy rating.

Infineon Technologies Results Do Not Impress

Fourth quarter results showed year-over-year growth of 1% and sequential growth of 6% to €3.94 billion. Segment results decreased 13.8% to €717 million as margins contracted 300 basis points.

In the Automotive segment, revenues declined by 2% to €1.92 billion as volume growth was offset by less favorable pricing. This also led to profits declining 15.7% as margins declined from 25.9% to 22.4%. Given the challenges that the automotive industry is facing, the stability in sales seems good. However, the sales included some sales being pulled into the quarter driven by the expiry of subsidies.

The Green Industrial Power segment saw an 8% decline to €463 million, driven by macroeconomic weakness that affected industrial demand and a weaker market for renewable energy, and as a result, fell 38% as margins declined from 22.1% to 14.9%.

Power & Sensor Systems sales increased 14% to €1.19 billion on tripling AI-related demand and an improved market environment for consumer electronics. Profits increased 23% to €179 million, with margins expanding from 14% to 15.1%.

Connected Secure Systems sales declined 9% to €369 million, driven by weakness in security and the Internet of Things or IoT. Profits declined 27% to €45 million as margins dropped 310 basis points to 12.2%.

Generally, the results were not impressive, as sales declines in security, IoT, renewables, and industrial could not offset improved demand patterns for data center power delivery and consumer electronics. That also removes some of the higher margin business from the mix, leading to a decline in margins.

Guidance Is Not A Bullish Guide Either

For the first quarter, Infineon is guiding for €3.6 billion in sales and mid-to-high teens margins. For the full year, modest sales growth is expected with margins in the high teens. As a reference, in FY25 the company reported €14.7 billion in sales and margins of 17.5%. Adjusted free cash flow was €1.8 billion and is expected to decline to €1.6 billion in FY26. I believe that with low growth and margins comparable to the FY25 results, as well as lower adjusted free cash flow, there are few bright spots. The only positive would be that free cash flow is expected to be €1.1 billion compared to a €1.1 billion free cash flow burn in the year prior, driven by the absence of free cash flow pressure from the acquisition of Marvell’s Automotive Ethernet business.

So, overall the guidance is not impressive, but we also note it includes a €400 million currency driven headwind.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Infineon Technologies

Currently we are seeing that macroeconomic headwinds are offsetting sales growth and eroding margins despite AI-powered delivery as a strong emerging sales driver. The other risk is the currency exchange rates.

Moving to growth drivers, the position that Infineon has for power semiconductors can be leveraged in power delivery to data centers, renewable energy, automation, connectivity, and the automotive market. Those are the long-term growth pillars for the company, as devices carry more chip content these days and power efficiency is becoming increasingly more important. For AI related power conversion where Infineon provides semiconductors from the grid to the CPU core, Infineon believes its products can drive system efficiency from 85% to 90%. This is quite important. Currently many data center operators are securing access to energy. However, for scalability, it is also important that system efficiency improves.

Infineon Technologies Fairly Valued, But Multiple Expansion Is Justified

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Since we are in FY26 and I believe looking ahead at expectations for FY27, the stock seems to be fairly valued against the company’s median EV/EBITDA multiple. However, I do believe that a higher multiple can be justified given the company’s AI growth opportunities. If we remain at some level of conservatism and value the stock at the average of the targets supported by expected results for FY26, we get to a price target of around $49, implying 16.5% upside. In a more bullish scenario, we factor in forward earnings of FY27, and that gives us a price target of around $58.50 and 39% upside.

I believe that margins are expected to improve in the years ahead with significant improvement in the cash conversion from EBITDA to FCF as CapEx as a percentage of operating cash flow is set to reduce substantially. Shareholder returns definitely do not seem impressive, but we note that debt is set to come down substantially through a combination of debt repayments and refinancing.

Conclusion: Infineon Technologies Remains Attractive For Long-Term Investors

The financial results and guidance of Infineon Technologies are not particularly exciting to me. However, I do believe that despite macroeconomic headwinds in some end markets there is substantial demand for power semiconductors and moreover for energy efficient ones. That is where Infineon Technologies excels and with AI and data center driven power demand, I do believe that Infineon Technologies has a strong investment case.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Automatic Data Processing: Double-Digit Dividend Hike Proves This Is A Dividend King Worth Owning
data.description:
Introduction

With the current state of the economy due to inflation, high-interest rates, potential AI disruptions, and a weakening labor market, it's quite refreshing to own dividend stocks. Particularly, companies with long pay histories, such as Dividend Aristocrats & Dividend Kings.

Especially, those who show their financial strength by raising their dividends double-digits, like Automatic Data Processing (ADP) did recently. This not only helps blunt the blow from inflation; this ensures the likelihood of seeing future increases from a strong, cash-flowing business.

In this article, I'll discuss ADP's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I'm initiating a buy rating on Automatic Data Processing.

Brief Overview

As previously mentioned, Automatic Data Processing is a Dividend King, meaning they have a long history of paying dividends. The company was founded in 1949 and went public over six decades ago in 1961.

ADP is a global payroll company that operates in over 140 countries. They allow businesses to (operate) efficiently by managing clients, personnel, tax information, and payroll for businesses of all sizes. ADP has over 1 million clients, which include behemoths like Amazon (AMZN) and Dell Technologies (DELL).

Latest Earnings

Automatic Data Processing reported their first quarter earnings for fiscal year 2026 on October 29th and managed to beat analysts' estimates on both their top and bottom lines.

EPS of $2.49 beat by a solid $0.04, while revenue exceeded expectations by $50 million, amounting to $5.18 billion. Both also increased from the previous quarter and year. EPS increased from $2.26 in Q4, and revenue from $5.13 billion.

Year-over-year, both rose 6.87% and 7.25%, respectively, solid considering the challenging economic backdrop. Despite their solid showing and growth, ADP's price is down nearly 17% in the past year.

Me, personally, I believe this is an overreaction from the market due to threats of AI disruption. However, ADP has been integrating AI into their business with the launch of ADP Assist, expected to drive growth by integrating AI capabilities into products like solving payroll anomalies.

The company has also been making acquisitions. The most recent acquisition was Pequity, a software management company launched in 2019. This is expected to broaden ADP's capabilities as well as help clients support compensation planning and make informed pay decisions.

In FY'25, they acquired WorkForce Software, expected to simplify client operations, increase visibility, and lower costs. So, from a fundamental standpoint, ADP seems to be firing on all cylinders.

Segment Performance

Automatic Data Processing currently operates in two segments: Employer Services & PEO Services. Both saw revenue growth of 7% despite margins declining double and triple-digits year-over-year.

Their Employer Services segment margin decline was related to their WorkForce Software acquisition, resulting in a (decline) of 50 basis points. This brought margins at the end of Q1 to 35.2%.

PEO's decline was due to increases in zero-margin benefit pass-through costs, workers' compensation & insurance, and higher marketing expenses. The segment's margins stood at 13%.

However, management still reaffirmed guidance for the full year. Revenue is expected to see 5% to 6% growth from $20.6 billion, while their bottom line is expected to see slightly higher growth from $10.01 in a range of 8% to 10%.

Double-Digit Dividend Hike

Despite potential AI disruptions, Automatic Data Processing showed its financial prowess with a strong 10.4% dividend hike earlier this week. This represented their 51st consecutive dividend increase, adding to their already impressive streak.

This also shows management's confidence in the business going forward. What's most impressive about ADP's dividend raise is this is amidst economic uncertainty and a time when other quality Dividend Kings have chosen to be more conservative.

Case in point, Dividend Kings Pepsi (PEP) & Procter & Gamble (PG) both raised their dividends a solid 5% earlier this year, lower than the prior year. And while this is likely to do with tariffs and economic uncertainty placing downward pressure on consumers, ADP's increase should give investors and shareholders confidence going forward.

Moreover, their dividends remain well-covered by their cash flows. For FY'25, ADP ended with a payout ratio of 50.4%. And strong cash flow growth and dividend raises are likely to continue, partly thanks to the company's frequent share repurchases.

Although buybacks have slowed a bit since 2022, likely due to inflation, the Dividend King has repurchased more than $1 billion in subsequent years. For 2025, they took 4.4 million shares off the books. This not only ensures the dividend will likely remain safe, but that cash flow and earnings will continue to see solid growth for the foreseeable future.

Balance Sheet

Automatic Data Processing's balance sheet was in good shape, as evidenced by their A credit rating. Additionally, the company has no debt maturing until 2028, with $4 billion worth of notes due.

Total long-term debt stood at $3.976, minimal in comparison to their market cap of over $100 billion. Liquidity was robust with nearly $2.5 billion in cash & equivalents.

Fairly Valued

According to Fast Graphs, ADP looks fairly valued here as they currently trade slightly below their normal P/E of 25.45x. The stock also trades near their 52-week low of $249.68.

So, for long-term investors, I think ADP is worth dollar-cost averaging into here and adding on any signs of further share price weakness. And the upside to their price target of $332 over the next 3 years is solid.

Risks & Bottom Line

Although the government shutdown seems to be ending soon, delayed impacts and the potential to see further economic weakening could result in more downside for Automatic Data Processing.

With the recent uptick in job losses, I suspect we could see a potential recession in the next year or two, as this is seemingly a sign of cracks starting to show. I personally believe even if we manage a soft landing, things will get worse before they get better, particularly in the labor market.

However, ADP continues to be one of the most resilient companies, and I expect despite share price pullbacks, they will continue to be a solid long-term wealth compounder.

data.title: Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
This note will highlight the results of the Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund for Q3’ 2025. It is published amidst the ongoing developments associated with the Russian war with Ukraine, ongoing inflation concerns in the U.S., Mideast violence, and significant government policy changes in the U.S. and elsewhere that are altering global trade and financial flows. Most equity indices worldwide delivered positive returns in the September quarter. Returns from bonds in the U.S. were positive.

Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund paid ordinary dividends of $0.335 per I share during Q3’2025, +8.8% versus Q3’24. For the trailing 12 months, your fund paid ordinary dividends of $1.27 per I share, +5.5% vs the prior year comparable period. The dividends per share were lower for A and C shares, to account for varying class-specific expenses. Your fund also paid capital gains dividends as follows in Q4’24: short-term capital gains of 5.5¢/share and long-term capital gains of 16.6¢/ share. Including the capital gains dividends, trailing 4-quarter dividends of $1.49 per I share were +24.2% vs the dividend of $1.20 per share for the 4 quarters ending September 30, 2024.

Approximately 90% of the dividends paid by your fund in 2024 were characterized as "Qualifying Dividends" for federal income tax purposes, leading to a lower tax rate on these payments. More than 80% of your Investment Income Builder’s equity holdings increased dividends in local currencies in 2024, a slightly higher percentage compared to the prior year. A stronger $US was a headwind to your fund’s dividend growth in calendar 2024. A weaker $US has been a tailwind this year since many of your fund’s equity holdings pay dividends in foreign currencies that we subsequently convert to dollars. Other factors may impact the distributable cash total.

The net asset value of Investment Income Builder’s I shares increased by +$5.24 per share ($26.87 to $32.11) over the trailing 12 months and increased by $1.85 per share ($30.26 to $32.11) during the September quarter. Your fund’s 9/30/25 net asset value was reduced by the aforementioned 22¢/share capital gains dividends paid in Q4’24. Investment Income Builder’s I share return of 7.24%% for Q3 2025 exceeded the blended benchmark return of 5.94% for the September quarter (the blended benchmark is 75% MSCI World Index and 25% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index). For the 12-month period ending September 30, 2025, Income Builder’s I share return of 25.96% exceeded the blended benchmark return of 13.59%. Performance comparisons of Investment Income Builder to its blended benchmark over various periods are shown on page 1.

The quarter ending September 30, 2025, was the 91st full calendar quarter since the inception of Thornburg Investment Income Builder in December 2002. In 67 of these quarters the fund delivered a positive total return. The fund has delivered positive total returns in 17 of its 22 calendar years of existence.

Importantly, for an investor reinvesting dividends Thornburg Investment Income Builder has delivered an average annualized total return of 10.18% (I shares extended) since its inception 22.75 years ago, split between quarterly income distributions and share price appreciation.

On a $100,000 initial investment a hypothetical investor owning Thornburg Investment Income Builder A shares and taking all ordinary dividends in cash (no reinvestment of quarterly dividends) would have received total cash dividends of $192,485 over these 91 quarters. In addition, the current value of the original investment stood at $289,652 as of September 30, 2025.

The market yield of the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index decreased from 7.06% to 6.69% during the September quarter (yield to lower of call price or maturity). On September 30, 2025, the $74 trillion Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index showed an average maturity of 8.3 years and a yield of 3.48%, the latter decreasing by -0.2% over the September quarter. Approximately 20% of portfolio assets were invested in interest-bearing debt and near-cash assets on September 30, 2025. The remaining 80% of portfolio assets were invested in equities as previously described.

The outlook for financial asset returns remains uncertain. Earnings and other valuation multiples for most segments of the U.S. equity market are above historic averages. The “real” yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury note (September 30 market yield on 10-year UST of 4.15% minus the August core Consumer Price Index of 3.1%) is positive at +1.05%. The average “real yield” on 10-year U.S. Treasury notes over the last 30 years was +1.25%. The absolute number of unemployed workers in the U.S. has been rising, making additional policy rate cuts probable despite inflation running above Federal Reserve targets. Recent progress to reach the Fed’s inflation targets has been difficult to achieve, complicated by uncertainty around tariffs and curtailments in the number of foreign-born workers in the U.S. labor force. A large supply of new government debt being issued plus ongoing corporate borrowing present challenges to further reductions in longer-maturity bond yields unless the U.S. economy weakens considerably in the coming quarters.

Incoming economic data from around the world indicate an uncertain global economy in the face of evolving U.S. trade policies, so far resisting falling into a recession. There are clear indications that global trade flows are being significantly altered by new U.S. trade policies. Winners and losers among multinational producers of tradeable goods will become obvious in time, but the current outlook for many global businesses is uncertain. Prices of traded financial assets will be volatile with day-to-day news changing perceptions of near-term economic performance, interest rate policies, and political decisions. We are optimistic about the future return potential of Thornburg Investment Income Builder’s assets. Why?

Virtually all the businesses in your portfolio retain their market positions, providing important products and services that generate cash flows to pay attractive dividends, today and in the future. We believe they are valued very attractively in relation to their own histories and relative to other assets.

The weighted harmonic average 2025 consensus expected price/earnings ratio for Thornburg Investment Income Builder’s equity portfolio, tabulated using FactSet data, was approximately 13.6x as of September 30, 2025, well below the 20.8x price/earnings ratio of the MSCI All Country World Index using 2025 expected EPS. Income Builder’s 4.3% weighted average equity portfolio dividend yield significantly exceeds the 1.8% dividend yield of the MSCI Index. We believe your Income Builder portfolio incorporates significant intrinsic value. The bond portion of your fund’s portfolio has a duration of approximately 4.1 years and a yield to maturity/call of approximately 7%.

data.title: Equinox Gold: Hitting The Sweet Spot In Mid-Tier Mining
data.description:
Among the gold companies I follow and cover, Equinox Gold (NYSE:EQX) has quietly become one of the more interesting mid-tier names in the space. In less than a decade, Equinox has grown from a single-asset miner into a diversified producers with a solid footprint across the Americas.

The portfolio now spans Canada, the U.S., Brazil, and Nicaragua — anchored of course by the Greenstone, Mesquite, Aurizona, and newly commissioned Valentine mines.

The June 2025 acquisition of Calibre Mining really changed the shape of Equinox in my opinion. It added the La Libertad and El Limón complexes in Nicaragua, along with the Valentine Mine in Canada — which poured first gold on the 14th of September. What this deal did was, is that it shifted Equinox's centre of gravity toward North America, lowered its cost base, and gave investors a clear line of sight to roughly one million ounces of annual production by 2026. However, a few of the older assets are still in flux — Castle Mountain remains in the permitting phase for its next expansion, while Los Filos has been moved into developments as Equinox reassess that asset's long-term potential.

Long-Term Trends

As can be expected for a young miner, production and margins have been somewhat volatile — but the last two years saw improving metrics on both accounts.

2025 is expected to be even better. Equinox has guided for 785koz-915koz of gold production in 2025 at an AISC of between $1,800/oz-$1,900/oz.

Portfolio Of Assets - Broken Down By Region

Canada

Greenstone

Greenstone is an open-pit mine in Ontario, which is expected to produce an average of 330k ounces of gold annually over its initial 15-year mine life - and will stand as Equinox's largest, and lowest-cost asset when running at full steam. The ramp-up in production has been progressing well. In Q3'25 Greenstone produced 56koz of gold at an AISC of $1,975/oz.

Equinox initially took a 60% stake in Greenstone in 2021, partnering with Orion, who held the remaining 40% stake. By May 2024, Equinox had bought out Orion to take full control, with the mine producing its first gold that same month.

Valentine

Valentine (which also formed part of the recent Calibre acquisition) is also set to become a really important asset in Equinox's portfolio. First gold was poured in September, and the mine is expected to reach nameplate capacity by the 2nd quarter of 2026.

Nicaragua

El Limon and La Libertad

El Limon and La Libertad were both acquired as part of the Calibre Acquisition — and together produced 71,119koz in Q3'25 at an AISC per ounce sold of $1,507.

Brazil

Aurizona

In April 2024, heavy rainfall at the Aurizona site triggered a "geotechnical event" that resulted in the plant standing idle for roughly eight weeks — a setback that required Equinox to revise its mine plan for this asset.

Recent production and cost trends have been uninspiring, but in the Q3 Earnings report, management remarked:

The Company is advancing permitting, exploration and studies related to an expansion that is expected to extend the mine life and increase annual gold production with development of an underground mine and satellite open pit deposits that would operate concurrently with the existing open pit mine.

Bahia Complex: Fazenda

Bahia Complex: Santa Luz

The graphs above for Fazenda and Santa Luz don't go all the way to Q3'25. That is because Equinox started reporting results from these two mines on a consolidated bases in 2025 — as the mines are situated near one another and share many synergies.

Year-to-date (at the end of Q3'25), the two mines produced a cumulative 31,503koz of gold at an AISC of $2,136 per ounce sold.

RDM

Equinox acquired the RDM asset through its acquisition of Leagold Mining in 2020.

In explaining RDM's volatile production profile, management explain on the company website that:

RDM experienced a number of operating disruptions in Q1 and Q2 2022 as the result of regulatory issues related to the expansion of the mine’s tailings storage facility. In July 2022, Equinox Gold made the decision to focus on processing low-grade stockpiles and perform owner mining to reduce costs while the tailings storage facility expansion is underway. As a result, RDM production was lower and costs higher in 2022 than in normal years. For 2023, Equinox Gold has resumed owner-operated mining but on a smaller scale than in previous years to reduce capital costs. Production at RDM in 2025 is forecast at 50,000 to 60,000 ounces of gold at all-in sustaining costs between $1,880 to $1,980 per ounce of gold sold.

USA

Mesquite Mine (California)

Mesquite was Equinox's first operating mine. Over the past decade, Mesquite has produced an average of approximately 125koz gold ounces annually. 2025 production is guided to come in between 90koz-105koz at an AISC of between $1,725-$1,825 per ounce.

Pan Mine (Nevada)

Pan Mine was acquired on the 17th of June 2025 — and contributed 11,877 gold ounces between 17 June and 30 September at an AISC per ounce sold of $1,629.

Growth Pipeline

I have already mentioned the Valentine Mine which is expected to ramp up to nameplate capacity by the 2nd quarter of 2026.

EQX has been making steady progress with its Castle Mountain expansion. During Q1, the company signed memorandums of understanding to start work on the environmental impact statement. However, the bigger development came in June, when Castle Mountain was officially accepted as a "FAST-41 project" by the U.S. Federal Permitting Council — a designation that should help streamline reviews and tighten up timelines. According to the current FAST-41 dashboard, federal permitting is expected to conclude by December 2026, which gives investors a more realistic window for when construction decisions might come into focus. It is still a long road ahead, but the project continues to move in the right direction — and with a bit more regulatory clarity now in hand.

Los Filos remains a bit more complicated, and probably the most politically sensitive asset in EQX's portfolio. Operations were suspended indefinitely in April after the land access agreement with the Carrizalillo community expired at the end of March. That was a critical setback since the CIL plant — central to the 2022 expansion plan — was meant to be built on Carrizalillo land. Without renewed access, the expansion is effectively on pause. Still, its not all dead in the water. In June, EQX reached new long-term agreements with the other two host communities, Mezcala and Xochipala, which at least keeps the door open to alternative development paths. Exploration work is set to continue in Q4, alongside engineering studies evaluating other possible sites for the carbon-in-leach plant. Any serious investment decisions will obviously hinge on those results.

Risks

Equinox's exciting growth story is not without risks. Like I mentioned above, Castle Mountain is still navigating regulatory approvals, while Los Filos faces ongoing community and permitting uncertainties that could delay future development. Operational disruptions, like the 2024 geotechnical event in Aurizona, highlight how environmental and weather-related factors can materially impact production and costs. What also concerns me a bit around EQX, is its somewhat shaky cost discipline — but I expect this to improve once the newer assets in Canada are fully up and running.

Valuation

I have selected a set of mid-tier gold producers as peers for Equinox to assess EQX's relative valuation against. These include B2Gold, Alamos Gold, IAMGOLD, Eldorado Gold, New Gold, SSR Mining, OceanaGold, Torex Gold, Regis Resources, and Northern Star Resources. I chose these peers because, like EQX, they operate primarily in the 300koz-1000koz annual production range, are actively developing growth projects, and face similar operational risks.

I like to look at FWD EV/EBITDA metrics when valuing miners as this tells me what I am paying for the underlying operations relative to the earnings they are forecast to generate.

Over the past five years, EQX traded at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 6% above its peers, on average.

Currently, EQX is trading at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio just 4% above the peer group average — suggesting to me that EQX is about fairly valued here, on a relative basis.

The Technical Take

Even with Equinox's recent strong run, the technicals still suggest a solid setup forming here. EQX is again trading above its 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving average, while the MACD indicator is flashing a bullish signal again.

Analyst Recommendations

9 out of 9 reporting analysts on the Bloomberg terminal have a "Buy" rating for Equinox — with an average 12-month price target that is 25.9% above where shares are currently trading.

Conclusion

I think Equinox is now one of the more compelling mid-tier producers. With Greenstone ramping up, and Valentine now pouring, I think the company is on track for roughly $1Moz annual production by 2026. Current valuation looks fair, and the technicals look enticing. I am initiating coverage with a "Buy" rating, and agree with the consensus $15.54 price target among wall street analysts — which suggests roughly 26% upside from current levels.

data.title: Shift4: Panic Sell Or Opportunistic Buy?
data.description:
Introduction

From my initial public coverage of Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR) in May 2024, Shift4 stock rallied from the $60s to the $120s by early 2025 on the back of continued robust business performance (supported by organic & inorganic expansion):

However, Shift4's announcement of a $2.5B acquisition of specialty payments and technology provider "Global Blue" in February 2025 triggered a massive sell-off in FOUR stock, which was then exacerbated by the "Liberation Day" broad market sell-off during April. While FOUR stock rebounded strongly along with the stock market in subsequent weeks over the summer, Shift4's Q2 2025 financial results and details of its $3.3B capital raise ($1.3B Senior Notes + $1B Convertible Preferred Stock (10M shares mandatory dilution) + $1B Floating Rate Term Loan) irked investors, leaving FOUR stock reeling in the $70s once again ahead of last week's quarterly report—wherein the fintech company missed headline estimates.

Now, in the immediate aftermath of Shift4's Q3 2025 report, FOUR temporarily dropped to a new 52-week low of ~$61 per share but has since rebounded back to the mid-$70s. In today's note, we shall review Shift4's Q3 2025 earnings report and reevaluate its long-term and near-term risk/reward to reach an informed investment decision. Let's go!

Shift4: By The Numbers (Q3 2025)

For Q3 2025, Shift4 marginally missed headline top- and bottom-line consensus estimates amid macro-induced consumer spending jitters, with revenue coming in at $1.18B (+29.4% y/y, miss by ~$4M) and non-GAAP EPS landing at $1.47 per share (miss by $0.01).

That said, Shift4's underlying business trends remained positive, with the company delivering healthy growth across end-to-end Payment Volume ($54.7B, up 26% y/y), GRLNF ($589.2M, +61% y/y), and adj. EBITDA ($292.1M, +56% y/y).

Now, while the step up in Shift4's financial performance in Q3 was primarily a function of inorganic growth brought on by the successful close of the Global Blue acquisition on 3rd July 2025, Shift4's organic GRLNF growth was also robust at +19% y/y.

Looking forward, Shift4's management narrowed FY-2025 guidance ranges -

  • Volume: $207-210B (+26-27% y/y) [from $200-220B]
  • GRLNF: $1.98-2.02B (+46-49% y/y) [from $1.965-2.035B]
  • Adj. EBITDA: $970-985M (+43-45% y/y) [from $960-990M]

with management highlighting a weakening consumer spending environment in core verticals -

In Q3 and early Q4 we experienced modestly worsening same-store-sales from our restaurant and hotel customers, but increased contribution from new verticals and geographies. As we’ve said in the past, while not immune to consumer spending levels, our massive cross-sell funnel affords us more resiliency during periods of economic uncertainty.

- Taylor Lauber, Shift4 CEO, in Q3 2025 shareholder letter.

As you may know, Shift4 acquired Global Blue to significantly expand its global reach and enhance its integrated payment offerings, particularly in luxury retail and travel sectors. Now, in recent months, Mr. Market has doled out a brutal punishment on Shift4's stock ever since the Global Blue acquisition was announced; however, this particular transaction has diversified Shift4's business mix—categorically and geographically—with exposure to the luxury retail sector reducing business volatility tied to consumer spending.

With Global Blue, Shift4 now has the opportunity to cross-sell to thousands of luxury retail customers. Now, while the bigger luxury brands like Louis Vuitton (OTCPK:LVMUY) are well-served by the likes of Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY) and Stripe (i.e., taking market share will be an uphill battle), Shift4 could certainly win several boutique firms on Global Blue's customer roster. As such, I am excited to follow this piece of the business in 2026.

As of Q3 2025, Shift4's contracted volume backlog stood at $35B, and its cross-sell payments funnel has expanded to >$1T. Over the medium term (3-4 years), Shift4 is targeting 30% CAGR growth in GRLNF and adj. EBITDA, with annual free cash flow projected to exceed $1B+ by 2028-29.

From a business standpoint, Shift4 has market-leading products in multiple categories. With international expansion still in early stages, Shift4's profitable growth story has many chapters left.

The Balance Sheet Concerns Are Overblown

Over recent quarters, Shift4 has advanced its international expansion plans via an aggressive M&A strategy that has also seen it acquire the likes of Vectron (Germany, Europe), Eigen (Canada), and SmartPay (New Zealand, Australia) in addition to the Global Blue acquisition.

As a result, Shift4's financial debt has ballooned to $4.8 billion as of the end of Q3 2025, which is roughly 5x 2025E adjusted EBITDA of $978M. Now, while this elevated leverage position is less than ideal due to higher interest costs, it is certainly not untenable.

For starters, Shift4 is a highly cash flow-generative business (FY-25E FCF: $488M, FY-28E FCF: $1B+), and the plan for Global Blue does not involve legacy business deprecation like past acquisitions, i.e., Global Blue's cash generation will support Shift4's future debt repayment efforts.

Now, with each M&A transaction, we have integration risks. And these risks are especially elevated in the Shift4-Global Blue combo due to cultural differences. However, Shift4's 27+ year history is laden with several M&A transactions, and I trust Jared Isaacman (now Executive Chairman), Taylor Lauber (CEO), and the Shift4 executive leadership team to deliver success once again!

Importantly, they have ample time to get things right. Having retired its 2026 Convertible Senior Notes [$450M] in Q2 2025 using some of the proceeds of its $3.3B capital raise, Shift4's debt repayment maturities are now staggered farther into the future.

Yes, Shift4 has a $690M debt repayment due in December 2025; the company should be able to cover it with cash (avoiding dilution from the converts). And, looking beyond that, Shift4 is expected to generate >$1B in annual FCF by 2028-29, which will be more than adequate to meet its longer-dated debt obligations.

Now, Shift4's recently-closed acquisition of SmartPay (closed on 4th November 2025, deal size ~$180M) and impending acquisition of Bambora (expected close Q1 2026, deal size unknown) aren't factored into the math here today; however, as I see it, Shift4's current liquidity and robust free cash flow generation provide adequate support for management's aggressive M&A push.

And Shift4's management and BOD seem to agree. After all, Shift4 just announced a new $1B share repurchase plan, focused on managing dilution.

At ~$73.18 per share (or $7.29B), Shift4 is trading at ~15x 2025E FCF, which is dirt cheap for a company growing as fast as Shift4. As such, I think the new stock buyback program makes total sense and should instill a high floor for FOUR stock.

Shift4 Fair Value And Expected Return

According to TQI's Valuation Model, FOUR stock is worth ~$136 per share, which is roughly 86% above its current level.

Additional Notes:

1) For the model, I have considered a $4.23B revenue base (2025E revenue) and assumed a reasonable 15% CAGR sales growth over the 2026-30 5-year modeling period.

2) While Shift4's FY-25E adj. FCF of $488M represents an adj. FCF margin of ~11.5%, I am sticking with our optimized FCF margin assumption of 15% due to an expectation of operational leverage and improved cash flow conversion for Shift4 at a larger business scale in the future.

3) Furthermore, to account for idiosyncratic risks tied to the integration of multiple M&A transactions (and consequent leverage position), I have increased the model's required IRR from 15% to 20%—thereby instilling a margin of safety in the model.

4) The share count has been raised to 99.6M shares, factoring in Shift4's $1B mandatory Convertible Series A Preferred Stock (FOUR.PR.A).

Based on a reasonable exit multiple of 20x P/FCF, I see Shift4 stock rising to $277.51 per share over the next five years at a CAGR rate of 30.55%.

With its 5-year expected CAGR return being significantly higher than TQI's investment hurdle rate of 15%, I continue to view Shift4 as an asymmetric investment for long-term investors at current levels.

FOUR Technical Setup

From a technical standpoint, Shift4 stock appears to be forming a massive triangle pattern. Now, triangles can break in either direction; however, with FOUR stocks rebounding off of the multi-year uptrend line (marked in green arrow) in recent days and weekly RSI levels indicating "oversold" conditions, I hold a constructive technical view on Shift4 stock.

Also, as per Elliot Wave Theory, Shift4 appears to have completed a 5-wave move to the downside when it tested the long-term support trendline in the low-$60s. Consequently, we can expect a move back up to the "wave-4" high around ~$90 per share in FOUR in the near-term.

And this constructive technical view is supported by Shift4's healthy business momentum and significant stock undervaluation.

Now, given heightened macroeconomic uncertainties, I remain open-minded here, but I really do like the odds of a bullish move.

Final Thoughts

Shift4 is primed for several years of rapid, profitable growth. The stock is deeply undervalued, and while its technical setup is directionally unclear, FOUR stock is sitting right above its multi-year bullish trendline. On the back of a ~40% dip off its early 2025 highs, I view Shift4's long-term risk/reward as truly asymmetric once again.

Key Takeaway: I continue to rate Shift4 a "Strong Buy" in the $70s.
